quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Rate of change modeling"?

options.text: Average value has the same units as f(x); the (1/(b-a)) factor divides out the length units
Finding a specific antiderivative by using a condition like F(0) = 3 to determine the value of C
If f'(x) = 0 for all x in (a,b), then f is constant on [a,b]; proven using MVT
f'(x) represents the instantaneous rate of change of whatever f(x) models: population, cost, temperature, concentration

quiz_questions.explanation: "Rate of change modeling" is defined as: f'(x) represents the instantaneous rate of change of whatever f(x) models: population, cost, temperature, concentration. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Equilibrium points"?

options.text: x-intercepts, y-intercept, local maxima and minima, inflection points, and behavior at asymptotes and endpoints
Sometimes algebraic simplification (canceling, expanding, dividing) before differentiation is easier than applying rules to complex expressions
A grid of short line segments showing the slope dy/dx = f(x,y) at each point; visual representation of a DE
Where f'(x) = 0: potential maxima, minima, or inflection points; represent equilibria in physical and economic models

quiz_questions.explanation: "Equilibrium points" is defined as: Where f'(x) = 0: potential maxima, minima, or inflection points; represent equilibria in physical and economic models. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Increasing/decreasing analysis"?

options.text: f'(x) > 0: f is increasing (quantity growing); f'(x) < 0: f is decreasing (quantity declining)
Uses the midpoint of each subinterval: M_n = Σf((xᵢ + xᵢ₊₁)/2)Δx; generally more accurate than left/right
Left-hand limit: lim(x→c⁻) f(x); right-hand limit: lim(x→c⁺) f(x); the two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits are equal
f' gives the topology (hills and valleys); f'' gives the shape (cupping up or down); together they determine the complete curve shape

quiz_questions.explanation: "Increasing/decreasing analysis" is defined as: f'(x) > 0: f is increasing (quantity growing); f'(x) < 0: f is decreasing (quantity declining). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Acceleration"?

options.text: f''(x) > 0: the rate of change is increasing (accelerating growth); f''(x) < 0: rate of change is decreasing (decelerating growth)
The tolerance for how close f(x) must be to L; represents the desired precision of the output
Motion along a straight line (one dimension); described completely by position as a function of time s(t)
First derivative test: always works but requires sign analysis on an interval; second derivative test: simpler but can be inconclusive

quiz_questions.explanation: "Acceleration" is defined as: f''(x) > 0: the rate of change is increasing (accelerating growth); f''(x) < 0: rate of change is decreasing (decelerating growth). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Sensitivity analysis"?

options.text: The derivative measures how sensitive the output is to changes in input: large |f'(x)| means small input changes cause large output changes
F(x) = P(X ≤ x) = ∫₋∞ˣ f(t)dt; F'(x) = f(x) by FTC; F ranges from 0 to 1
= e^(f(x)) · f'(x); the most common chain rule application with exponentials
Priority for choosing u: Logarithmic > Inverse trig > Algebraic > Trigonometric > Exponential; u is differentiated, dv is integrated

quiz_questions.explanation: "Sensitivity analysis" is defined as: The derivative measures how sensitive the output is to changes in input: large |f'(x)| means small input changes cause large output changes. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f'(x) represents the instantaneous rate of change of whatever f(x) models: population, cost, temperature, concentration"?

options.text: Epsilon (ε)
Conservative forces
Rate of change modeling
d/dx[cos x] = -sin x

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Rate of change modeling".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Where f'(x) = 0: potential maxima, minima, or inflection points; represent equilibria in physical and economic models"?

options.text: Restricting domain
Rectilinear motion
Equilibrium points
Justification language

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Equilibrium points".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f'(x) > 0: f is increasing (quantity growing); f'(x) < 0: f is decreasing (quantity declining)"?

options.text: Increasing/decreasing analysis
Combining with u-substitution
Closed interval method
Newton's method

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Increasing/decreasing analysis".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f''(x) > 0: the rate of change is increasing (accelerating growth); f''(x) < 0: rate of change is decreasing (decelerating growth)"?

options.text: Exponential function
Acceleration
Combined technique
Justification language

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Acceleration".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The derivative measures how sensitive the output is to changes in input: large |f'(x)| means small input changes cause large output changes"?

options.text: One-sided limits
Sensitivity analysis
Inflection point at P = K/2
d/dx[cos x] = -sin x

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Sensitivity analysis".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Modeling with Derivatives is accurate?

options.text: These derivatives are essential for modeling growth, decay, and natural phenomena.
The derivative of an inverse function is (f⁻¹)'(a) = 1/f'(f⁻¹(a)), derived from the chain rule applied to f(f⁻¹(x)) = x.
Understanding growth rates is essential for comparing algorithms, modeling populations, and analyzing asymptotic behavior.
Modeling with Derivatives uses derivative analysis to understand real-world phenomena modeled by functions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Modeling with Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Modeling with Derivatives?

options.text: Common scenarios include expanding/contracting shapes, Pythagorean relationships, trigonometric relationships, and similar triangles.
Derivatives represent rates of change in any context: population growth rates, velocity, temperature change rates, marginal economic quantities, and reaction rates.
Average Value of a Function extends the concept of arithmetic mean to continuous functions.
Work against gravity: W = ∫mgy'(x)dx for lifting objects along curves.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Modeling with Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Modeling with Derivatives?

options.text: Setting the derivative equal to zero finds critical points (equilibria, optimal values).
Arc Length and Surface Area uses integration to measure curve length and rotational surface area.
Linear approximation uses the tangent line to estimate function values near a known point: f(x) ≈ f(a) + f'(a)(x-a).
Understanding growth rates is essential for comparing algorithms, modeling populations, and analyzing asymptotic behavior.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Modeling with Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Modeling with Derivatives?

options.text: The power rule: d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n.
Arc length of y = f(x) from a to b: L = ∫ₐᵇ √(1 + [f'(x)]²)dx.
The sign of the derivative indicates whether the quantity is increasing or decreasing, and the second derivative reveals acceleration or deceleration of change.
Arc Length and Surface Area uses integration to measure curve length and rotational surface area.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Modeling with Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Modeling with Derivatives?

options.text: Choose u and dv from the integrand using the LIATE priority: Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential (choose u from earlier in the list).
Modeling with Derivatives uses derivative analysis to understand real-world phenomena modeled by functions.
Basic antiderivative rules: ∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (n ≠ -1), ∫eˣdx = eˣ + C, ∫(1/x)dx = ln|x| + C, ∫cos x dx = sin x + C, ∫sin x dx = -cos x + C.
Continuous compound interest follows A = Pe^(rt).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Modeling with Derivatives as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Modeling with Derivatives?

options.text: Engineering error analysis: propagation of measurement uncertainty through formulas using differentials to estimate how input errors affect computed results
Financial mathematics: differentiating revenue functions R(x) to find marginal revenue and determine the optimal quantity to maximize profit
Quality control: setting an acceptable error margin (ε) for a product property and determining the input precision (δ) needed in the manufacturing process
Epidemiological modeling: the derivative of infection count I'(t) gives the rate of new cases; the inflection point of I(t) indicates when the outbreak is peaking

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Modeling with Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Modeling with Derivatives?

options.text: Market saturation: adoption of new technology (smartphones, streaming services) follows an S-curve as the product approaches market saturation
Financial markets: the 'Greeks' (delta, gamma, theta) in options pricing are derivatives that measure how option value changes with respect to stock price, rate of change, and time
Understanding S-curves in product adoption: inflection point marks the transition from rapid early adoption growth to market saturation deceleration
Engineering stress analysis: the derivative of deflection in a loaded beam reveals points of maximum stress where structural failure is likely

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Modeling with Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Modeling with Derivatives applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Species population modeling: deer populations in managed forests follow logistic growth, with K determined by food and habitat availability
Market analysis: marginal revenue R'(x) = 0 identifies the production level that maximizes total revenue for a monopolistic firm
Market saturation: adoption of new technology (smartphones, streaming services) follows an S-curve as the product approaches market saturation
Orbital mechanics: computing orbital periods and trajectories involves integrals with expressions like √(a²-x²), solved with trigonometric substitution

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Modeling with Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Modeling with Derivatives?

options.text: Epidemiological modeling: the derivative of infection count I'(t) gives the rate of new cases; the inflection point of I(t) indicates when the outbreak is peaking
Ladder sliding down a wall: if the base moves away at 2 ft/s, how fast does the top slide down? Classic Pythagorean related rates problem used in construction safety
Scientific measurement: using increasingly precise instruments to estimate a true value—smaller measurement intervals approximate the 'limit' of the quantity
AP exam problems frequently ask students to apply MVT to piecewise data: 'Given the table, must there be a value c where f'(c) = 3?'

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Modeling with Derivatives.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Modeling with Derivatives in practice?

options.text: Bacterial growth: E. coli doubles every 20 minutes in ideal conditions; 1 bacterium becomes ~70 billion in 12 hours (2³⁶ ≈ 6.9 × 10¹⁰)
Engineering stress analysis: the derivative of deflection in a loaded beam reveals points of maximum stress where structural failure is likely
Algorithm analysis in computer science: O(n log n) sorting algorithms (merge sort) outperform O(n²) algorithms (bubble sort) for large datasets
Battery charge level: if I(t) is the current flow, the charge Q(x) = ∫₀ˣ I(t)dt represents the accumulated charge at time x

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Modeling with Derivatives.
